title: Optimization
blurb: How networks actually learn: GD/SGD/Momentum/RMSprop/Adam, overfitting vs underfitting, the regularization toolbox (L1/L2, weight decay, dropout, early stopping), batch normalization, weight initialization, and transfer learning. The densest source of exam points.

knowledge_points.title: Optimization Algorithms (GD, SGD, Momentum, RMSprop, Adam)
knowledge_points.recap: **GD** updates $\theta_{t+1}=\theta_t-\alpha\frac{\partial L}{\partial\theta_t}$ once per epoch (full dataset). **SGD** updates every mini-batch — faster, noisier (helps escape saddle points). **Momentum** accumulates a velocity to push through local minima/saddles. **RMSprop** adapts a per-parameter learning rate from gradient magnitudes. **Adam = Momentum + RMSprop** with bias correction. We always step in the **negative** gradient direction (steepest descent).

questions.q: What is the main difference between gradient descent (GD) and stochastic gradient descent (SGD)? Name two advantages of SGD.
questions.answer:
**Difference:** GD performs the optimizer step **at the end of each epoch** (gradient over the whole training set); SGD performs a step **at every iteration / mini-batch**.
**Advantages of SGD:** (1) more optimization steps per unit time → **converges faster**; (2) the **noisier** updates help **escape saddle points / local minima**.
questions.extend: Noise is a feature, not a bug: SGD's stochasticity is regularizing and helps escape flat/saddle regions where full-batch GD stalls.

questions.q: What two optimizers does Adam combine? Why is SGD+Momentum usually better than plain SGD?
questions.answer:
**Adam = RMSprop + Momentum** (adaptive per-parameter rates + velocity), plus bias correction.
**SGD+Momentum** is usually better because momentum helps the optimizer **escape saddle points** and accelerate along consistent gradient directions, smoothing out noisy updates.
questions.extend: Momentum = exponential moving average of gradients (velocity). RMSprop = EMA of squared gradients (per-parameter scaling). Adam fuses both.

questions.q: What is the bias correction in Adam (and why is it needed)? Do Adam with and without bias correction have different global minima?
questions.answer:
Adam initializes the first/second moment estimates $m,v$ at **zero**, so early estimates are **biased toward zero**, making the first updates too small/ineffective. **Bias correction** $\hat m=\frac{m}{1-\beta_1^k}$, $\hat v=\frac{v}{1-\beta_2^k}$ rescales them so early steps are effective.
**Global minimum:** unchanged (the loss/goal is the same) — bias correction only makes early convergence faster, though the path may end in a **different local minimum** (SS24 2.9; Summary IV.4 Q1e: *False* they differ in global minimum).
questions.extend: Bias correction matters most in the first few steps (when $\beta^k$ is far from 0). RMSprop has no bias correction simply because Adam introduced it later.

questions.q: Why can we call RMSprop an *adaptive* optimizer? Why do we always step in the negative gradient direction, and how would you modify SGD to *maximize* the loss?
questions.answer:
**Adaptive:** RMSprop adapts the learning rate **per parameter**, based on the running magnitude of that parameter's gradients (large gradients → smaller effective step).
**Negative gradient:** the gradient points in the direction of steepest **ascent**; to minimize we go opposite. To **maximize**, flip the sign: $\theta_{t+1}=\theta_t + \alpha\frac{\partial L}{\partial\theta_t}$ (gradient ascent).
questions.extend: Adaptive methods (RMSprop/Adam) normalize each dimension's step, so you don't need to hand-tune a single global rate for differently-scaled parameters.

questions.q: In which cases would you usually reduce the learning rate when training a neural network?

options.text: When the training loss stops decreasing.
To reduce memory consumption.
After increasing the mini-batch size.
After reducing the mini-batch size.

questions.answer: Reduce the LR **when training loss plateaus** (LR scheduling/decay to fine-tune) and **after reducing the mini-batch size** (smaller batches → noisier gradients → a smaller step is safer). LR has nothing to do with memory; larger batches actually permit *larger* (not smaller) rates.
questions.extend: Batch size and learning rate scale together (roughly linearly): smaller batch → noisier → lower LR; larger batch → smoother → can raise LR.

questions.q: Which are affected by multiplying the loss function by a constant positive value when using SGD?

options.text: Memory consumption during training.
Magnitude of the gradient step.
Location of minima.
Number of mini-batches per epoch.

questions.answer: Only the **magnitude of the gradient step** changes — scaling $L$ by $c$ scales $\nabla L$ by $c$, so the step size scales too (equivalent to scaling the learning rate). The **location** of the minima, memory, and batch count are unaffected.
questions.extend: Scaling the loss ≡ scaling the LR. The argmin doesn't move, but your effective step does — a subtle but common exam point.

questions.q: (a) Why use larger mini-batches in SGD? (b) Why do we usually use small mini-batches in practice? (c) Name an optimizer using both first and second order moments.
questions.answer:
(a) Larger mini-batches make the **gradients less noisy** (better estimate of the true gradient).
(b) We use small mini-batches because of **limited GPU memory** (note: 'faster computation' alone scores 0).
(c) **Adam** (first moment = momentum, second moment = RMSprop-style).
questions.extend: Batch-size trade-off: bigger = smoother gradients but more memory & sometimes worse generalization; smaller = noisier (regularizing) and memory-friendly.

questions.q: You switch from SGD to Adam and training loss drops fast but validation generalization is slightly worse. Give a plausible reason and a common remedy.
questions.answer: Adam's adaptive per-parameter steps can converge to **sharper minima** that generalize a little worse than SGD+momentum's flatter solutions. Remedies: use **SGD+momentum with a schedule** for the final phase, add **weight decay** (AdamW), or tune the learning rate / use warmup.
questions.extend: A known empirical pattern: Adam trains faster, SGD+momentum often generalizes better on vision. AdamW (decoupled weight decay) narrows the gap.

questions.q: Your gradient updates are oscillating. Which actions might help stabilize training?
questions.answer: **Decrease the learning rate** (smaller, steadier steps) and **try other optimizers** (e.g. Adam with adaptive/momentum behaviour). Increasing the LR or decreasing the batch size would make oscillation **worse** (bigger/noisier steps).
questions.extend: Oscillation ⇒ steps too large/noisy ⇒ lower LR, add momentum/adaptivity. Smaller batches add noise, not stability.

options.text: Decrease the learning rate
Increase the learning rate
Decrease the batch size
Try other optimizers

questions.q: For $L(\theta)=\theta^2-6\theta+9$ with Newton's update $\theta_{t+1}=\theta_t-H^{-1}\nabla L$, $\theta_0=0$: find $\theta_1,\theta_2$.
questions.answer: $\nabla L=2\theta-6$, $H=2$. $\theta_1=0-\tfrac{1}{2}(-6)=\mathbf{3}$; $\theta_2=3-\tfrac{1}{2}(2\cdot3-6)=\mathbf{3}$. Newton's method reaches the minimum of a quadratic in **one step** ($\theta^\*=3$).
questions.extend: Second-order (Newton) optimization solves a quadratic exactly in one step — the appeal, and why it's too expensive for big nets (needs the Hessian).

questions.q: Name the two techniques Adam combines and how each improves on plain SGD.
questions.answer: **Momentum** — uses an exponential moving average of past gradients to **smooth updates** and accelerate convergence. **RMSProp** — adapts a **per-parameter learning rate** using an EMA of squared gradients (variance). Adam fuses both (plus bias correction).
questions.extend: Adam = momentum (1st moment) + RMSProp (2nd moment). Smooth direction × adaptive per-parameter scale.

questions.q: Identify the optimizer from each pseudocode: **Alg1** updates $b_t\!\leftarrow\!\mu b_{t-1}+g_t,\ \theta_t\!\leftarrow\!\theta_{t-1}-\gamma b_t$; **Alg2** uses $v_t\!\leftarrow\!\alpha v_{t-1}+(1-\alpha)g_t^2,\ \theta_t\!\leftarrow\!\theta_{t-1}-\gamma g_t/(\sqrt{v_t}+\epsilon)$; **Alg3** keeps $m_t,v_t$ with $\beta_1,\beta_2$ and bias-corrected $\tilde m_t,\tilde v_t$.
questions.answer: **Alg1 = GD/SGD with momentum** ($\mu$ is the momentum coefficient; $b_t$ is a running average of gradients). **Alg2 = RMSprop** (EMA of squared gradients re-scales each step). **Alg3 = Adam** (first + second moment EMAs with bias correction).
questions.extend: Recognize by the state: velocity only ⇒ momentum; squared-grad EMA ⇒ RMSprop; both + bias correction ⇒ Adam.

questions.q: (6.2) What is the major difference between the momentum optimizer (Alg1) and RMSprop (Alg2), and the idea behind it? (6.3) What happens when the momentum coefficient $\mu>0$ in Alg1?
questions.answer: **6.2:** RMSprop introduces **second-moment (squared-gradient / variance) information** to **re-scale each update by its variance**, dampening high-variance directions — Alg1 uses only a running average of gradients (first moment). **6.3:** With $\mu>0$, Alg1 **accumulates a moving average of gradients (momentum)** → it **reduces oscillations and speeds up convergence**, but can **overshoot / diverge** past minima.
questions.extend: Momentum smooths the *direction* (1st moment); RMSprop adapts the *scale* (2nd moment). Adam fuses both.

questions.q: Explain the $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ hyperparameters of Adam.
questions.answer: $\beta_1$ is the **decay-rate coefficient of the running average of the first moment** (gradient mean); $\beta_2$ is the **decay-rate coefficient of the running average of the second moment** (squared gradients / variance). They control how much past vs current gradient information is retained.
questions.extend: Higher $\beta$ ⇒ longer memory of past gradients. (Making $m,v$ biased is a *consequence*, not the purpose, of $\beta$.)

questions.q: What is the major difference between Newton's method and the first-order optimizers above, and why is it not widely used for deep learning?
questions.answer: Newton's method is **second-order** — it uses the **Hessian** (curvature), not just the gradient. It is impractical for deep learning because **computing and inverting the Hessian** over millions of parameters is **prohibitively expensive in compute and memory**.
questions.extend: Second-order = exact curvature (one-step on quadratics) but $O(\text{params}^2)$ Hessian ⇒ infeasible at scale.

questions.q: Explain the concept behind momentum in SGD.
questions.answer: Momentum **accumulates a (weighted) running average of past gradients** (a velocity) and steps along it. This **accelerates** progress in consistent directions and helps the optimizer **roll through saddle points / flat regions** where the raw gradient is small.
questions.extend: Momentum = EMA of gradients ⇒ inertia through saddles and faster, smoother descent.

questions.q: Which optimizer introduced in the lecture uses the second but not the first order moment?
questions.answer: **RMSProp** — it keeps an exponentially-decaying average of **squared** gradients (second moment) to scale per-parameter steps, but does **not** accumulate a first-moment (momentum) term.
questions.extend: RMSProp = 2nd moment only; Adam = 1st + 2nd moments.

questions.q: Why is Newton's method not commonly used to train deep models (1p)? What is an advantage of using it (1p)?
questions.answer: **Not used:** it needs the **Hessian** (second derivatives) and its inverse — **computationally too expensive** for models with many parameters. **Advantage:** it **converges in fewer iterations** and **needs no learning rate** (curvature sets the step).
questions.extend: Second-order = curvature-aware, LR-free, few steps — but the Hessian is infeasible at scale.

questions.q:
(5.1) Name two limitations of SGD that momentum can solve and how. (5.2) In $
u_{k+1}= eta
u_k-\alpha
abla_	heta L$, what do $\alpha$ and $ eta$ represent?
questions.answer: **5.1** Momentum fixes: **slow learning / small steps** (it builds velocity in consistent directions → faster) and **getting stuck in local minima / saddle points / noise** (accumulated velocity carries through, and the EMA reduces noise). **5.2** $\alpha$ = **learning rate**; $\beta$ = **momentum / velocity-accumulation (friction) coefficient**.
questions.extend: Momentum = EMA of gradients with rate $\beta$, scaled step $\alpha$ → speed + saddle escape + denoising.

questions.q: How does Nesterov momentum differ from standard momentum?
questions.answer: Nesterov takes a **look-ahead step**: it first jumps in the direction of the **accumulated momentum**, then computes the gradient **at that look-ahead position**. This lets the gradient **correct overshooting of the momentum within the same step**, giving more responsive updates.
questions.extend: Standard: gradient at current point, then add momentum. Nesterov: peek ahead via momentum, then correct.

questions.q: Is RMSProp a first- or second-order method (1p)? What is the main difference between RMSProp and SGD+Momentum?
questions.answer: **First order** (it only uses gradients, no Hessian). **Difference:** RMSProp uses the **second moment** — an exponentially-decaying average of **squared** gradients (variance) — to dampen oscillation and scale per-parameter steps; **SGD+Momentum** accumulates the **first moment** (a running average of the gradients).
questions.extend: Both first-order; RMSProp scales by gradient variance, momentum averages gradient direction.

questions.q: Which statement(s) about batch size and SGD is/are true?
questions.answer: **True:** *increasing the batch size reduces the noise in SGD* (more samples → lower-variance gradient), and *batch size can have a regularization-like effect* (small batches add noise). **False:** SGD needs *more* update steps than GD (not fewer); and a larger batch does not *always* improve performance.
questions.extend: Bigger batch = less gradient noise; small-batch noise can act like regularization.

options.text: Increasing the batch size reduces the noise in SGD.
Compared to GD, SGD typically requires fewer update steps.
A larger batch size always leads to better performance.
The batch size can have an effect similar to regularization.

questions.q: Momentum SGD: $m_{t+1}=\beta m_t-\alpha\frac{\partial L}{\partial\theta_t},\ \theta_{t+1}=\theta_t+m_{t+1}$. (1) Name $\alpha,\beta$. (2) Name two problems of plain SGD and how momentum helps.
questions.answer: **$\alpha$** = learning rate; **$\beta$** = momentum / accumulation coefficient. Momentum helps with: **oscillations** (averaging gradients cancels noise, smoothing the path), **slow convergence** (builds velocity through flat areas), and **saddle points** (inertia rolls through zero-gradient regions).
questions.extend: Momentum's velocity term denoises, accelerates, and carries the optimizer through saddles.

questions.q: Which statements about deep-learning optimizers are true?
questions.answer: **True:** *RMSProp requires less memory than Adam* (one moment vs two), and *Adam uses the 1st and 2nd moments* of gradients. **False:** RMSProp is not 'SGD+momentum with LR divided by an EMA of gradients', and it does not estimate the Hessian.
questions.extend: RMSProp = 2nd moment only (lighter); Adam = 1st + 2nd moments (heavier).

options.text: RMSProp is equivalent to SGD with momentum and LR divided by an EMA of gradients.
RMSProp employs a local estimation of the Hessian.
RMSProp requires less memory than Adam.
Adam optimizer uses 1st and 2nd moment of gradients.

questions.q: Explain the $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ hyperparameters in the Adam optimizer.
questions.answer: $\beta_1$ = decay-rate coefficient of the **moving average of the first moment** (the mean of recent gradients). $\beta_2$ = decay-rate coefficient of the **moving average of the second moment** (the variance / squared gradients).
questions.extend: Higher $\beta$ ⇒ longer memory; $\beta_1$ governs momentum, $\beta_2$ governs the adaptive scaling.

questions.q:
Explain the purpose and benefit of the 'bias correction' step in Adam, given $m_{k+1}= eta_1 m_k+(1- eta_1)
abla L$ and $v_{k+1}= eta_2 v_k+(1- eta_2)(
abla L\odot
abla L)$.
questions.answer: $m,v$ are **initialized to 0**, so early estimates are **biased toward zero** (too small). **Bias correction** ($\hat m=m/(1-\beta_1^k),\ \hat v=v/(1-\beta_2^k)$) rescales them to **unbiased** estimates, giving **effective early updates** and accelerating early training.
questions.extend: Zero-init biases the moving averages low; dividing by $1-\beta^k$ de-biases them for the first steps.

knowledge_points.title: Overfitting & Underfitting
knowledge_points.recap: **Overfitting:** great on training data, poor on unseen data (train loss ≪ val loss; val loss rises). **Underfitting:** poor even on training data (both losses high/plateau). The one-word go-to fix for overfitting: **regularization**. Detect via the train/val loss curves.

questions.q: Define overfitting and underfitting in one sentence each. In a single word, what is the go-to solution to overfitting? How do the loss curves look?
questions.answer: **Overfitting:** the model performs too well on training data but fails to generalize to unseen data (train loss keeps dropping while val loss rises). **Underfitting:** the model fails to perform well even on training data (both curves stay high / plateau). **Go-to solution:** **Regularization**.
questions.extend: Curve signatures: overfitting = diverging train/val gap (val turns up); underfitting = both high and flat. Label your axes & both curves to earn the marks.

questions.q: Which statements about loss curves, overfitting and underfitting are correct?

options.text: Underfitting is indicated by high training loss and high validation loss.
Overfitting occurs when both training and validation loss are high.
A model is underfitting if training loss is low and validation loss is high.
Consistently decreasing train AND validation loss indicates overfitting.

questions.answer: Only **'underfitting = high train + high val loss'** is correct. Overfitting is **low train, high val** (a growing gap); both-decreasing curves indicate **healthy** training, not overfitting.
questions.extend: Map the 2×2: low/low = good, low/high = overfit, high/high = underfit, high/low = (usually a bug or distribution mismatch).

questions.q: State two behaviours that indicate underfitting. State a possible reason for validation loss being *lower* than training loss.
questions.answer:
**Underfitting signs:** the training loss is still decreasing when training stops (not trained enough/too low capacity); the **training accuracy is very low**.
**Val < train loss reasons:** the validation set is **easier** than the training set; the model is still in the **underfitting** phase early in training; or a **bug** (e.g. dropout/augmentation active in training only).
questions.extend: Cross-links to Ch I: dropout & augmentation are *on* during training and *off* at validation, which can legitimately push val loss below train loss.

questions.q: Your cat-species classifier gets low training error but high validation error. What is happening and what helps?
questions.answer: Classic **overfitting** (memorizing training data, failing to generalize). Remedies: more data / **data augmentation**, **regularization** (dropout, weight decay), early stopping, or a smaller model.
questions.extend: Low-train/high-val is the textbook overfitting signature. Attack it with regularization + more (or augmented) data.

questions.q: Train loss = 0.05, validation loss = 0.9 and rising. Name the regime and two fixes that do NOT change the dataset.
questions.answer: **Overfitting.** Data-free fixes: add **regularization** (dropout / weight decay), use **early stopping**, or **reduce model capacity**.
questions.extend: Low-train / high-val = overfit. The no-new-data levers are regularization, early stopping, smaller model.

questions.q: After 60 epochs the training loss keeps decreasing but the validation loss starts rising. Name the phenomenon and two fixes.
questions.answer: **Overfitting.** Any two fixes: **early stopping**, **L1/L2 regularization / weight decay**, **dropout**, **data augmentation**, **Batch/Layer Norm** to stabilize, or **collect more data**.
questions.extend: Rising val loss + falling train loss = textbook overfitting; the fixes are the whole regularization toolbox.

questions.q: Training for a while, the training loss keeps decreasing but the validation loss is increasing. Which methods can optimize this issue?
questions.answer: This is **overfitting**. **Early stopping** and a **learning-rate schedule** help. **Increasing the learning rate** or **removing data augmentation** would make overfitting **worse**.
questions.extend: Falling train + rising val = overfitting; cure with early stopping / regularization, not by removing augmentation.

options.text: Early-stopping.
Increase the learning rate.
Use a learning-rate schedule.
Remove data augmentation.

questions.q: Two CNNs are trained with the same optimizer/hyperparameters. (4.1) After 200 epochs, Model 1 has high loss on **both** train and val — which is worse, what is the phenomenon, how to improve it? (4.2) After adding layers to Model 1, its **validation** loss rises while train falls — what is the phenomenon? (4.3) Which model would you deploy, and what technique gives the best performance?
questions.answer: **4.1** Model 1 is **underfitting** (too low capacity) → increase parameters/capacity, tune hyperparameters, add BatchNorm, normalize inputs. **4.2** Now Model 1 **overfits**. **4.3** Deploy the model with the **lowest validation loss** and use **early stopping** (keep the epoch with best val loss) to avoid the overfitting tail.
questions.extend: Underfit ⇒ add capacity; overfit ⇒ regularize + early stopping. Deployment choice is driven by validation loss.

questions.q: Your cat-species classifier gets a low training error but a high test error. Which are promising things to try?
questions.answer: **Get more training data** and **add weight regularization** — both reduce the **generalization gap** (this is overfitting). A *bigger* network or a *different initialization* would not address overfitting (a bigger net can make it worse).
questions.extend: Low train / high test = overfitting ⇒ more data or regularization, not more capacity.

options.text: Use a bigger neural network.
Get more training data.
Try a different initialization during training.
Add weight regularization.

questions.q: Explain overfitting and underfitting (1p each) and describe how the loss curves look in each case.
questions.answer: **Underfitting:** low model capacity / training too slow → **both** train and val loss stay **high** with a small gap (both could keep descending). **Overfitting:** the model memorizes the data and generalizes poorly → train loss keeps **decreasing** while val loss **decreases then rises** after a critical point.
questions.extend: Underfit = high error both curves; overfit = diverging curves (train↓, val↑).

questions.q: Two students train image classifiers. (7.1) Erika's curves show train loss falling while val loss rises — interpret and how to improve. (7.2) Max's curves show both losses high/flat — interpret and how to improve.
questions.answer: **7.1 Overfitting** → apply **regularization** (dropout, L1/L2/weight decay, data augmentation), **early stopping**, or **reduce capacity**. **7.2 Underfitting** (or poor optimization) → **increase model capacity**, or fix optimization: **decrease/decay the learning rate**, use Adam/BN.
questions.extend: Train↓ val↑ = overfit (regularize); both high = underfit (more capacity / better optimization).

questions.q: Identify the technique in this pseudocode and explain the counter/patience logic: it tracks `best_validation_loss`, resets `counter=0` on improvement, else `counter+=1`, and breaks when `counter>=patience`.
questions.answer: It implements **early stopping**. It monitors the **validation loss**, remembering the best value; each epoch **without improvement increments a counter**, and once the loss hasn't improved for `patience` consecutive epochs the loop **breaks** — stopping training before it overfits.
questions.extend: Patience = grace epochs of no val-improvement before stopping; resets whenever val loss hits a new best.

questions.q: Training accuracy is high but validation accuracy is much lower. (1) Name the phenomenon. (2) If you reduce the model size, what happens to the train–val gap and why?
questions.answer: (1) **Overfitting** (poor generalization). (2) The **gap shrinks**: a smaller model has **lower capacity**, so it can **memorize the training data less**, reducing the difference between training and validation accuracy.
questions.extend: Overfit gap ∝ capacity; shrinking the model is a (capacity-reducing) regularizer.

questions.q: Plain SGD with L2 was run with four learning rates [3e-4, 4e-1, 2e-5, 8e-3] giving loss curves; one diverges, one barely moves, one is noisy/high, one descends smoothly. How do you match LR to curve behaviour?
questions.answer: The **largest LR (4e-1)** makes the loss **diverge/oscillate** (way too high). **2e-5** is **too low** → the loss barely decreases. **8e-3** is **too high** → unstable/high loss. **3e-4** is the **good** LR → smooth, steady descent.
questions.extend: Read curves by stability: diverging = LR too high, flat = too low, smooth descent = well-tuned.

knowledge_points.title: Regularization (L1/L2, Weight Decay, Dropout, Early Stopping)
knowledge_points.recap: A regularization term makes training *harder* to force generalization. **L1** pushes weights to exactly zero (sparsity); **L2** shrinks all weights toward (but not to) zero. **Weight decay** is L2 applied in the optimizer step (scaled by LR). **Dropout** randomly drops neurons during training (an ensemble); at inference scale by $p_{keep}$ (or use inverse dropout during training). **Data augmentation** is regularization; **early stopping** is debatable (it just halts, doesn't make training harder).

questions.q: What is the effect of L1 vs L2 regularization on the weights? What are the differences between L1/L2 regularization terms and 'weight decay'?
questions.answer:
**L1** pushes some weights to **exactly zero** → sparse weights. **L2** spreads the penalty so **all** weights become smaller, but not necessarily zero.
**vs weight decay:** (1) L1/L2 terms are added to the **loss function**, whereas weight decay is added directly to the **optimizer step**; (2) in weight decay the term is also scaled by the **learning rate**, mitigating the effect of its magnitude.
questions.extend: L1 → feature selection / sparsity; L2 → smooth small weights. Weight decay ≈ L2 but decoupled into the update rule (this is the AdamW distinction).

questions.q: Explain how dropout works during training and inference (mind the definition of $p$). How do we avoid dropout's computational overhead at inference? Why is dropout an ensemble?
questions.answer:
**Training:** at each iteration, drop neurons/channels with probability $p_{drop}$. **Inference:** keep all neurons but scale activations by $p_{keep}$: $x^\star = x\cdot p_{keep}$ (to match expected magnitudes).
**Avoid overhead:** use **inverse dropout** — scale the surviving neurons by $1/p_{keep}$ already **during training**, so inference needs no scaling.
**Ensemble:** each dropout mask defines a different sub-network, so training with dropout ≈ training an **ensemble** of exponentially many shared-weight networks.
questions.extend: $p_{keep}=1-p_{drop}$. Inverse dropout is what frameworks use so test-time is a plain forward pass.

questions.q: Why is data augmentation a regularization technique? Why is early stopping *not* really one?
questions.answer:
**Data augmentation** virtually increases the training-set size and variety, making training **harder** and forcing the model to learn more general patterns → regularization.
**Early stopping** doesn't make training harder; it merely **halts** when the generalization gap starts to grow. It doesn't fix overfitting, just prevents the current weights from getting worse (saving checkpoints is a better approach).
questions.extend: Regularization (in the strict I2DL definition) = anything that makes training *harder* to improve generalization. Augmentation qualifies; early stopping is borderline.

questions.q: The term 'regularization term' is ambiguous — what are its two usages?
questions.answer: (1) A term that makes training **harder**, forcing the network to generalize better (the go-to exam definition). (2) A term representing an **additional objective** added to the training process (e.g. a task-specific penalty).
questions.extend: Usage (1) is the generalization sense (dropout, L2, augmentation); usage (2) is the 'extra loss term' sense (e.g. KL term in a VAE).

questions.q: Dropout...

options.text: ... makes your network train faster.
... can be seen as an ensemble of networks.
... is an efficient way for regularization.
... has trouble with tanh activations.

questions.answer: Dropout **is an ensemble of (sub-)networks** and an **efficient regularizer**. It does **not** speed up training (it can slow convergence), and it is activation-agnostic (no special problem with tanh).
questions.extend: Each mini-batch trains a different randomly-thinned network; averaging them at test time (via $p_{keep}$ scaling) is the ensemble effect.

questions.q: Why is L2-regularization often called 'weight decay'? Derive the update rule.
questions.answer: Adding $\frac{\lambda}{2}\lVert\theta\rVert^2$ to the loss gives gradient contribution $\lambda\theta$, so the GD update becomes $\theta_{k+1}=\theta_k-\alpha(\nabla L(\theta_k)+\lambda\theta_k)=(1-\alpha\lambda)\theta_k-\alpha\nabla L(\theta_k)$. The factor $(1-\alpha\lambda)<1$ **shrinks ('decays') the weights** every step — hence 'weight decay'.
questions.extend: The $(1-\alpha\lambda)$ multiplicative shrink is the literal 'decay'. This is exactly L2 for vanilla SGD (decoupled in AdamW).

questions.q: You apply dropout with $p_{drop}=0.5$ but forget to scale at inference (no inverse dropout). What happens to the outputs and how do you fix it?
questions.answer: At inference all neurons are active, so the expected pre-activations are roughly **2× too large** (since only half were active during training). Fix by **scaling activations by $p_{keep}=0.5$ at test time**, or use **inverse dropout** (scale by $1/p_{keep}$ during training) so test-time needs no change.
questions.extend: The scaling keeps the expected activation magnitude consistent between train and test — skip it and every downstream layer sees an unexpected distribution shift.

questions.q: Your weights are sparse (most near 0, a few very large). How does L2 regularization change this distribution, and what's the benefit?
questions.answer: L2 makes the weights **more evenly distributed**, penalizing large weights so none dominate. Benefit: the model **uses more of its capacity** and generalizes better (less reliance on a few large weights).
questions.extend: L2 spreads weight magnitude (shrinks large weights); L1 would instead push many weights to exactly 0 (sparsity).

questions.q: Which techniques help reduce overfitting?
questions.answer: **True:** dropout, data augmentation, and L2 weight decay all regularize. **False:** increasing model capacity generally **increases** overfitting risk.
questions.extend: Regularization toolbox: dropout, augmentation, weight decay, early stopping. Bigger model ⇒ more overfitting.

options.text: Dropout.
Data augmentation.
Increasing the model's capacity.
L2 weight decay.

questions.q: Regularization: which statements are true?
questions.answer: **True:** it is *any technique that aims to reduce the **generalization gap***, and *weight decay (L2) spreads the decision power among as many neurons as possible*. **False:** it does not aim to *increase training accuracy*; and *ReLU is not a regularization technique* (so the dropout/ReLU/early-stopping claim is false).
questions.extend: Regularization shrinks the train–val gap (often at some training-accuracy cost); ReLU is an activation, not regularization.

options.text: Any technique that reduces validation error and increases training accuracy.
Any technique that aims to reduce the generalization gap.
Dropout, ReLU activations, and early stopping can all be considered regularization.
Weight decay (L2) spreads the decision power among as many neurons as possible.

questions.q: What is true about Dropout?
questions.answer: **True:** it *can be seen as an ensemble of networks* (random sub-networks) and is an *efficient way to regularize*. **False:** it does not make the network train faster, and it has no special trouble with tanh activations.
questions.extend: Dropout = cheap ensemble regularizer; it slows (not speeds) convergence and is activation-agnostic.

options.text: ...makes your network train faster.
...can be seen as an ensemble of networks.
...is an efficient way for regularization.
...has trouble with tanh activations.

questions.q: Your segmentation network is too large for the task. Name two approaches to counteract overfitting **without** changing the convolutional layers.
questions.answer: Any two: **weight regularization / weight decay**, **data augmentation**, or **dropout**. (Adding more data also helps.) Changing BN, learning rate, skip connections, or pooling is not an answer here.
questions.extend: Too-large model ⇒ overfits ⇒ regularize (weight decay / dropout) or augment, leaving the conv stack intact.

questions.q: Regularization & dropout: which statements are true?
questions.answer: **Regularization** is used to **improve generalization to unseen data** (not to speed convergence, learn more complex patterns, or reduce both errors). **Dropout** is **a regularization strategy**; it is **inactive at inference**, $p_{drop}=0.5$ does **not** mean half-capacity at inference, and it **reduces** (not increases) neuron co-adaptation.
questions.extend: Regularization targets the generalization gap; dropout is train-only and decreases co-adaptation.

options.text: Regularization speeds up convergence during training.
Regularization improves generalization to unseen data.
Dropout is considered a regularization strategy.
Dropout remains active during the inference phase.

questions.q: Explain why dropout is considered an ensemble method (training vs inference).
questions.answer: During **training**, randomly disabling neurons trains a different **sub-network** each step — effectively training a huge ensemble of sub-networks sharing weights. At **inference**, the **full network** (with appropriate scaling) **approximates the average** of all these sub-networks' predictions.
questions.extend: Dropout = implicit weight-shared ensemble; test-time full net ≈ averaging the ensemble.

questions.q: Which penalty-based regularization encourages a network to focus on a small number of important features? Explain.
questions.answer: **L1 regularization** — it penalizes the absolute weight values, **encouraging sparsity** (driving many weights to exactly 0), so the model effectively **selects a small set of important features**.
questions.extend: L1 → sparse weights (feature selection); L2 → small, spread-out weights.

questions.q: (7.1) Name a regularization method that encourages sparse weights and write its formula for $W_{m\times n}$. (7.4) How can sparsity in weight matrices be leveraged for lossy model compression, and what are the practical advantages?
questions.answer: **7.1 L1 regularization (Lasso):** $\|W\|_1=\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}|w_{ij}|$. **7.4** **Set weights below a threshold to zero** and store the result as **sparse matrices** → **less storage** and **more efficient compute** (skip the zeros).
questions.extend: L1 drives weights to exactly 0 → prune + store sparsely → smaller, faster models.

questions.q: Which statements about dropout regularization are true?
questions.answer: **True:** it *prevents overfitting by randomly zeroing a fraction of neurons during training*, and it *promotes learning more generic features* (less memorization). **False:** it is not 'adding noise to the input', and it is **not** CNN-only.
questions.extend: Dropout randomly masks neurons in training → discourages memorization/co-adaptation across all net types.

options.text: Dropout adds noise to the input data during training.
Dropout randomly sets a fraction of neurons to zero during training.
Dropout is only applicable to CNNs.
Dropout helps prevent memorizing specific samples, promoting generic features.

questions.q: (4.2) Define L2 regularization and why it is used. (4.3) After training with L2 ($\lambda=1$), the weight histogram shows all weights close to 0 — why?
questions.answer: **4.2** $\text{reg}=\lambda\sum_i\theta_i^2$ — it penalizes large weights, **spreading the decision power** across many small weights to **reduce overfitting / improve generalization**. **4.3** The **regularization coefficient $\lambda$ is too high**, so the penalty dominates and **shrinks all weights toward 0** (hence the name 'weight decay').
questions.extend: L2 shrinks weights; too-large $\lambda$ over-shrinks everything toward zero (under-uses capacity).

knowledge_points.title: Batch Normalization
knowledge_points.recap: BN normalizes a layer's activations: $y=\gamma\,x_{norm}+\beta$ with learnable $\gamma,\beta$ (length = #channels/features). It stabilizes/ speeds training, reduces sensitivity to initialization, mitigates vanishing/exploding gradients, and mildly regularizes. At **test time** it uses **running (cached) mean & variance** from training since there's no batch. In a CNN (shape B×C×H×W) statistics are per-channel → $\gamma,\beta\in\mathbb{R}^{C}$.

questions.q: Why is it useful to apply batch normalization after a linear (FC/conv) layer? What optimization problems does it help with?
questions.answer: BN normalizes the layer inputs so each layer receives **stable, well-scaled** activations (mitigating internal covariate shift). Benefits: **faster/more stable training**, **less sensitivity to initialization**, efficient backprop, and it helps with the **vanishing/exploding gradient** problems by keeping activations from blowing up or shrinking.
questions.extend: BN keeps activations zero-mean/unit-variance per channel before the learnable rescale — the same 'keep signals in a healthy range' motivation as input normalization (Ch I).

questions.q: What are $\gamma$ and $\beta$ in batch normalization and what is their purpose? How can BN represent the identity (undo normalization)?
questions.answer: $\gamma$ (scale) and $\beta$ (shift) are **learnable parameters** that let the network **undo** the normalization if needed: $y=\gamma\,x_{norm}+\beta$. Setting $\gamma=\sqrt{\text{Var}(x)}$ (i.e. $\sigma$) and $\beta=\mu$ (the original mean) recovers $y=x$ — the identity. Their purpose is to preserve the layer's representational power.
questions.extend: Without $\gamma,\beta$, BN would force every layer's output to zero-mean/unit-variance, removing useful scale/shift. The learnable pair restores flexibility, including the identity.

questions.q: How is BN applied at training vs test time? Which variables must be cached and why (for a CNN input B×C×H×W)?
questions.answer:
**Training:** normalize using the **current mini-batch** mean & variance, then apply $\gamma,\beta$; also update **running averages** of mean/variance.
**Test:** use the **cached running mean & variance** (a single sample has no batch to compute statistics from), giving deterministic, consistent outputs.
**Cached variables (CNN):** mean and variance of shape $[C]$ (per channel), since BN in a CNN normalizes over B,H,W per channel.
questions.extend: Per-channel stats: for B×C×H×W, BN reduces over (B,H,W) → C means and C variances. Caching them avoids test-time dependence on batch composition.

questions.q: How is batch normalization applied differently between a fully-connected and a convolutional layer? How many learnable parameters does BN have?
questions.answer:
**FC layer:** normalize over the batch for **each neuron** → statistics per feature; $\gamma,\beta$ have length $D$ (number of features).
**Conv layer:** normalize over batch **and spatial dims** for **each channel** → statistics per channel; $\gamma,\beta$ have length $C$ (number of channels).
Learnable parameters $=2\times(\text{features or channels})$ (one $\gamma$ and one $\beta$ each).
questions.extend: Key difference: conv-BN shares stats across all spatial positions of a channel (translation consistency), FC-BN is per-neuron.

questions.q: What is the consequence of a small batch size on a batch-normalization layer? Why is BN sometimes called a regularizer?
questions.answer:
With a **small batch**, the mini-batch mean/variance are **noisy/inaccurate** estimates of the true statistics, so normalization becomes unstable and training quality degrades.
That same noise is why BN **mildly regularizes**: small-batch statistical noise injects randomness into the updates (like a weak dropout), improving generalization — but BN shouldn't be your primary regularizer.
questions.extend: Small-batch BN is unreliable → use GroupNorm/LayerNorm when batches must be tiny. The noise is a side-effect, not a substitute for dropout/weight decay.

questions.q: For BN $y=\gamma\,x_{norm}+\beta$ after an FC layer with output $8\times16$: what are the dimensions of $\gamma,\beta$, and what are $\frac{\partial L}{\partial\gamma},\frac{\partial L}{\partial\beta}$?
questions.answer:
$\gamma,\beta\in\mathbb{R}^{1\times16}$ (one per feature). Gradients (summing over the batch of $N=8$):
$\frac{\partial L}{\partial\gamma}=\sum_n \big(\text{dout}\odot x_{norm}\big) = $ `np.sum(dout * x_norm, axis=0)`,
$\frac{\partial L}{\partial\beta}=\sum_n \text{dout} = $ `np.sum(dout, axis=0)`.
questions.extend: Because $\gamma,\beta$ are broadcast across the batch, their gradients sum the upstream gradient over the batch dimension (axis 0).

questions.q: Why does Batch Normalization behave differently at train vs test time, and what breaks if you wrongly use batch statistics at test time?
questions.answer: **Train** uses the current mini-batch mean/variance (and updates running averages); **test** uses the **cached running statistics**. Using batch stats at test time makes a single sample's output **depend on the rest of its batch** → non-deterministic, inconsistent predictions.
questions.extend: Cache running $\mu,\sigma$ during training; at inference there is no batch to estimate from.

questions.q: Why can Batch-Norm behave poorly when the mini-batch contains only one sample ($m=1$)?
questions.answer: With $m=1$ the batch mean equals the sample and the **variance is 0**, so normalizing $\frac{x-\mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2+\epsilon}}$ is meaningless/unstable (variance in the denominator) and the centered value is always 0 → information loss. Single-sample statistics also don't represent the dataset.
questions.extend: BN needs a batch big enough for meaningful statistics; for tiny batches use Layer/Group Norm instead.

questions.q: Which of the following adds learnable parameters to the network?
questions.answer: **Batch Normalization** (learnable $\gamma,\beta$). **Dropout**, **weight decay**, and **weight initialization** add **no** learnable parameters.
questions.extend: Only BN among these has trainable parameters; dropout/weight-decay/init are parameter-free mechanisms.

options.text: Dropout
Batch Normalization
Weight Decay
Weight Initialization

questions.q: A convolutional layer outputs shape $N,H,W,C$ and batch normalization is applied. Across which dimension(s) is the normalization performed? (Equivalently, state the shape of the mean array.)
questions.answer: Normalization is performed **across $N,H,W$** — one mean/variance **per channel** — so the **mean array has shape $(1,1,1,C)$**. Each channel is normalized using statistics pooled over the batch and the spatial dimensions.
questions.extend: Conv BN = per-channel: pool over batch + spatial; FC BN = per-feature (pool over batch only).

questions.q: Given input $x$, which identities are always true? (For dropout, assume the same neurons are chosen.)
questions.answer: **True:** $\text{Dropout}(\text{ReLU}(x))\equiv\text{ReLU}(\text{Dropout}(x))$ and $\text{MaxPool}(\text{ReLU}(x))\equiv\text{ReLU}(\text{MaxPool}(x))$ — ReLU is monotonic and commutes with masking/max. **False:** BN does not commute with ReLU, and Sigmoid/ReLU don't commute.
questions.extend: ReLU (monotone, elementwise) commutes with max-pool and with a fixed dropout mask, but not with BN or sigmoid.

options.text: BatchNorm(ReLU(x)) ≡ ReLU(BatchNorm(x))
Dropout(ReLU(x)) ≡ ReLU(Dropout(x))
MaxPool(ReLU(x)) ≡ ReLU(MaxPool(x))
ReLU(Sigmoid(x)) ≡ Sigmoid(ReLU(x))

questions.q: What is true about batch normalization (train and test set from the same distribution)?
questions.answer: **True:** BN *makes gradients more stable so we can train deeper networks*, and BN *has learnable parameters* ($\gamma,\beta$). **False:** BN can be used with dropout; and at **test** time it uses **running (training) statistics**, not test-batch statistics.
questions.extend: BN: learnable $\gamma,\beta$; train uses batch stats, test uses running averages.

options.text: Batch normalization cannot be used together with dropout.
Batch normalization makes the gradients more stable, so we can train deeper networks.
At test time, BN uses mean/variance computed on test-set samples.
Batch normalization has learnable parameters.

questions.q: Explain the main difference in how a BatchNorm layer is used in a convolutional vs a fully-connected network.
questions.answer: In a **conv** network BN normalizes **per channel** — statistics are pooled over the batch **and the spatial dimensions**, giving one $(\gamma,\beta)$ per channel. In an **FC** network BN normalizes **per feature** (statistics over the batch only).
questions.extend: Conv BN = per-channel (batch+spatial); FC BN = per-feature (batch only).

questions.q: Which statements about batch normalization are true?
questions.answer: **True:** it *reduces internal covariate shift* and *can be applied per channel in CNNs*. **False:** it works **worse** (not better) for small mini-batches, and it **has** learnable parameters ($\gamma,\beta$).
questions.extend: BN: per-channel in CNNs, learnable $\gamma,\beta$, needs a reasonably large batch.

options.text: Reduces internal covariate shift.
Works better for smaller mini-batch sizes.
Has no learnable parameters.
Can be applied per channel in CNNs.

questions.q: BN with $\hat x=\frac{x-E[x]}{\sqrt{\text{Var}[x]+\epsilon}},\ y=\gamma\hat x+\beta$. (g) Why is a very small mini-batch bad for BN? (h) How are mean/variance obtained at inference and why?
questions.answer: **(g)** With a small batch the mean/variance are **noisy and unrepresentative** of the full data, so normalization is unreliable. **(h)** At inference we use a **running (moving) average** of the mean/variance collected during training — this keeps the output **deterministic**, independent of which other samples share the batch.
questions.extend: Small batch ⇒ noisy stats; test-time running averages make BN deterministic and batch-independent.

questions.q: What $\gamma,\beta$ would BN need to learn to recover the original unnormalized input ($y=x$)?
questions.answer: $\gamma=\sqrt{\text{Var}[x]+\epsilon}$ and $\beta=E[x]$ — these exactly invert the normalization, so $y=\gamma\hat x+\beta=x$.
questions.extend: BN can represent the identity; the affine $\gamma,\beta$ let it undo normalization if that's optimal.

questions.q: Which statements about Batch Normalization are true?
questions.answer: **True:** BN *accelerates the training process in most cases* and *reduces the impact of poor weight initialization*. **False:** it does not speed up a single batch's raw processing time, and it is **not** skipped at test time (it uses running statistics).
questions.extend: BN speeds convergence and tolerates bad init; at test time it runs with stored statistics.

options.text: Batch Normalization speeds up the processing time of a single batch.
Batch Normalization accelerates the training process in most cases.
Batch Normalization layers are always skipped at test time.
Batch Normalization reduces the impact of poor weight initialization.

knowledge_points.title: Weight Initialization (Xavier / He)
knowledge_points.recap: Good init keeps activation/gradient variance stable across layers. **Xavier/Glorot** suits tanh/sigmoid; **He/Kaiming** $W\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\frac{2}{n_{in}})$ suits ReLU and prevents dead units. Never initialize all weights to the **same value** (symmetry → all neurons learn identically).

questions.q: What weight-initialization scheme fits ReLU, and how does it affect the activations? What variance does Xavier give?
questions.answer: **He/Kaiming initialization** $W\sim\mathcal{N}\!\big(0,\frac{2}{n_{in}}\big)$ (a ReLU-tuned variant of Xavier) keeps the **variance of activations stable across layers** and prevents the **dead-ReLU** problem. Xavier keeps $\text{Var}(X_L)=\text{Var}(X_{L+1})$ so signal magnitude is preserved forward (and gradients backward).
questions.extend: ReLU halves the variance (zeros out negatives), so He uses $2/n_{in}$ to compensate; Xavier ($1/n_{in}$-style) is for symmetric activations (tanh).

questions.q: What is true about Xavier initialization? (Each FC layer: input $X$, weights $W$, bias $b$.)

options.text: $\text{Var}(X_L) = \text{Var}(X_{L+1})$
$\text{Var}(W_{L-1}) = \text{Var}(W_L)$
$\text{Var}(X_L) = \text{Var}(W_L)$
$\text{Var}(W_L)=\frac{1}{m}$, where $m$ is the number of columns of $W_L$.

questions.answer: The defining property is **$\text{Var}(X_L)=\text{Var}(X_{L+1})$** — Xavier preserves activation variance from layer to layer. The other statements misstate which variances are equal or the exact $\frac{1}{n}$ factor (it's based on $n_{in}$/fan-in, not 'columns').
questions.extend: Xavier's goal: signal neither explodes nor vanishes as it flows through layers. That's a statement about **activation** variance across layers, not weight variances.

questions.q: What happens if we initialize all weights of the model to the same value?
questions.answer: **Symmetry is never broken:** neurons in a layer receive identical gradients and update identically, so they all learn the **same features** — the network cannot learn complex patterns. Weights must be sampled **randomly** from some distribution.
questions.extend: Random init breaks symmetry so different neurons specialize. All-equal (or all-zero) weights collapse a layer to a single effective neuron.

questions.q: You initialize all weights of a ReLU MLP to 0. Describe what happens during training, and what He initialization fixes.
questions.answer: With all-zero (or all-equal) weights, every neuron computes the same output and gets **identical gradients** → symmetry never breaks, so the layer learns nothing. **He init** $W\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\frac{2}{n_{in}})$ samples random weights (breaking symmetry) and keeps activation variance stable for ReLU.
questions.extend: Zero/equal init = dead symmetry; random init breaks it; He scales the variance specifically for ReLU.

questions.q: Which statements about weight initialization are true?
questions.answer: **True:** Batch norm reduces the impact of poor initialization; **Xavier doesn't work well with ReLU**; **Kaiming considers the input-layer size only**. **False:** 'Kaiming considers both input and output sizes' (that's the Xavier flavour).
questions.extend: Xavier ↔ tanh/sigmoid (fan-in & fan-out); Kaiming/He ↔ ReLU (fan-in only, variance $2/n_{in}$).

options.text: Batch normalization reduces the impact of poor weight initialization.
Kaiming initialization considers both input and output layer sizes.
Xavier initialization doesn't work well with ReLU activations.
Kaiming initialization considers the size of the input layer only.

questions.q: Briefly explain weight initialization. Name one bad method and why it's bad, plus two common strategies.
questions.answer: **Concept:** initialize weight matrices with **random non-zero** values. **Bad:** **zero-initialization** — it doesn't **break symmetry**, so all neurons get identical gradients and learn the same thing. **Common strategies:** **He/Kaiming** (for ReLU) and **Xavier** (for tanh/sigmoid).
questions.extend: Init must break symmetry; He↔ReLU (fan-in), Xavier↔tanh/sigmoid (fan-in+out).

questions.q: A network uses Kaiming init with ReLU. You replace all ReLUs with Sigmoids and change nothing else. What problem appears and how do you fix it?
questions.answer: **Kaiming init is designed for ReLU**; with Sigmoid it no longer preserves the activation variance across layers (and sigmoids in middle layers cause **vanishing gradients**). **Fix:** use **Xavier initialization** (suited to sigmoid/tanh) — or switch back to ReLU / add BatchNorm.
questions.extend: Init must match the activation: He/Kaiming↔ReLU, Xavier↔sigmoid/tanh.

knowledge_points.title: Hyperparameter Tuning & Learning Rate
knowledge_points.recap: **Grid search** is simple but its cost explodes with the search-space size (curse of dimensionality) — random search is often more efficient. **Learning rate** is the key hyperparameter: too high → divergence; too low → painfully slow convergence. LR schedules decay the rate over training.

questions.q: Name a problem from a learning rate that is too high, and one from a rate that is too low. What is the main bottleneck of grid search?
questions.answer:
**Too high:** the cost function does not converge and can even **diverge**. **Too low:** it converges very slowly (or gets stuck), taking a very long time.
**Grid search bottleneck:** the search space grows exponentially with the number of hyperparameters, and each configuration needs a full (expensive) training run — impractical for large spaces.
questions.extend: LR is the single most important hyperparameter. Grid search scales as (values)^(params) → use random/Bayesian search for many hyperparameters.

questions.q: What is a saddle point and the problem with GD there? How does SGD help?
questions.answer: At a **saddle point** the gradient is (near) **zero** but it is not a minimum, so plain GD **gets stuck** (no update direction). **SGD's noisier updates** can knock the optimizer out of the saddle/flat region and let it continue descending.
questions.extend: High-dimensional loss surfaces have far more saddles than local minima; noise (SGD) and momentum are the standard escapes.

questions.q: You have 4 hyperparameters with 5 candidate values each. How many runs does full grid search need, and what cheaper strategy is often just as good?
questions.answer: $5^4=\mathbf{625}$ runs. **Random search** over the same budget is often as good or better, because not all hyperparameters matter equally — it samples more distinct values of the important ones.
questions.extend: Grid search scales exponentially in #hyperparameters; random/Bayesian search scales far better.

questions.q: For GPU-memory reasons you decrease your mini-batch size. Should you increase or decrease your learning rate (1p)? Why (1p)?
questions.answer: **Decrease** the learning rate. A smaller mini-batch is **less representative of the full data** — its gradient estimate has **higher variance / more noise** — so a large LR on noisy gradients risks **overshooting / diverging**; lowering the LR keeps updates stable.
questions.extend: Smaller batch ⇒ noisier gradient ⇒ smaller, safer steps. (Linear-scaling rule: LR roughly scales with batch size.)

questions.q: A scheduler computes `get_lr = base_lr * gamma ** (epoch // n)`. What class is this (1p)? Explain its role during training (1p) and give two variants (2p).
questions.answer: It is a **(step) learning-rate scheduler** (`StepLR`). **Role:** it **decays the learning rate by a factor `gamma` every `n` epochs**, which stabilizes training, avoids overshooting / oscillation around the minimum, and helps converge better. **Two variants (any):** `ExponentialLR`, `CosineAnnealingLR`, `MultiStepLR`, `PolynomialLR`, `LinearLR`, `CyclicLR`, `OneCycleLR`, `ReduceLROnPlateau`, `LambdaLR`.
questions.extend: Step decay drops LR in stages; cosine/exponential/plateau schedulers vary how the LR is annealed over training.

questions.q: Grid-searching three learning rates $\tau_1,\tau_2,\tau_3$ for vanilla SGD gives three training-accuracy curves. The official answer is $\tau_1>\tau_3>\tau_2$. Explain the reasoning that orders learning rates from these curves.
questions.answer: A **larger LR** makes training accuracy **rise faster initially** but, if too large, plateaus lower or oscillates; a **smaller LR** rises **slowly and steadily**. Reading the curves: the fastest, stable climber is the largest stable LR ($\tau_1$), the slowest climber is the smallest ($\tau_2$), leaving the intermediate one ($\tau_3$) in between → $\tau_1>\tau_3>\tau_2$.
questions.extend: Larger LR ⇒ faster but rougher convergence; smaller LR ⇒ slower but smoother. Map curve speed/stability to LR magnitude.

questions.q: Name a disadvantage of a small mini-batch size and a disadvantage of a large mini-batch size.
questions.answer: **Small batch:** **noisy / high-variance** gradient updates → slower, less stable training (more steps). **Large batch:** **requires more memory** (heavy GPU usage) and can get stuck near saddle points / sharp minima.
questions.extend: Batch size trades gradient noise (small) against memory and generalization (large).

questions.q: For vanilla SGD you grid-search three learning rates with converged training-accuracy curves and order them $\tau_1>\tau_3>\tau_2$. Separately, a training-loss plot shows a sudden drop — name the technique that produced it.
questions.answer: **Ordering:** the largest stable LR ($\tau_1$) climbs fastest, the smallest ($\tau_2$) slowest, giving $\tau_1>\tau_3>\tau_2$. **Sudden loss drop:** **learning-rate scheduling / step decay** (reducing the LR mid-training).
questions.extend: Faster, higher curve ⇒ larger LR; a step-down in the loss curve ⇒ an LR-decay schedule.

questions.q: For the convex objective $\min_{x}x^2$: (5.5) the optimum? (5.6) with $x_0=5$, lr $=1$, how many GD steps to converge (no momentum)? (5.7) starting from a random $x_0$, how could you speed up GD (no momentum)?
questions.answer: **5.5** $x^\*=0$. **5.6** It **never converges** — the gradient is $2x$, so the update $x\leftarrow x-1\cdot2x=-x$ just **oscillates** $5\to-5\to5\dots$ (the LR is too large). **5.7** **Reduce / decay the learning rate** (adaptive LR, line search, or gradient clipping).
questions.extend: $\nabla=2x$ with lr 1 flips the sign each step (overshoot); a smaller/decaying LR converges.

questions.q: Name one problem from a learning rate that is too high, and one from a learning rate that is too low.
questions.answer: **Too high:** the loss may **diverge (blow up) or oscillate** around the minimum without converging. **Too low:** convergence is **extremely slow**, or the model gets **trapped in a poor local minimum**.
questions.extend: LR too high → overshoot/diverge; too low → crawl / stuck. Tune or schedule between the extremes.

knowledge_points.title: Transfer Learning
knowledge_points.recap: Reuse a model pre-trained on a large dataset as a backbone, then fine-tune on a smaller target dataset. Early layers learn generic low-level features (reusable); later layers are task-specific. Useful when labeled target data is limited.

questions.q: Describe a scenario from the exercises where transfer learning was used, and how.
questions.answer:
**Autoencoder pre-training:** train an autoencoder **unsupervised** to reconstruct a large *unlabeled* dataset, yielding an encoder that is a good **feature extractor**. Then remove the decoder, attach a small **classifier**, and train it on the smaller **labeled** dataset — transferring the representation learned from the big dataset.
(Also: use a pre-trained network as a **backbone** for a segmentation/classification task and fine-tune.)
questions.extend: Two flavours: self-supervised pre-training (autoencoder) → fine-tune; or take a pretrained backbone, replace the head, freeze early layers, train on new data.

questions.q: Which statements about transfer learning are true?

options.text: It always involves fine-tuning all layers of the pre-trained model.
It can be applied to tasks with limited labeled data.
A pre-trained model used in the new task should be trained on similar data.
It can significantly reduce training time for a new task.

questions.answer: **True:** transfer learning helps when **labeled data is limited** and **reduces training time**. **False:** you often **freeze** early layers (not fine-tune all), and the source data doesn't have to be *similar* — early-layer features (edges/textures) are generic and transfer broadly.
questions.extend: Common recipe: freeze the pretrained backbone, replace the final layer to match new classes, train just the head (cheap), optionally unfreeze for fine-tuning.

questions.q: How do you adapt a pre-trained AlexNet (1000-class output) as a backbone for a 3-class classifier efficiently? Would you reuse a car-brand classifier for an animal task?
questions.answer:
**Adapt AlexNet:** resize inputs to its expected size; **replace the last dense layer** with one matching 3 classes; **freeze** the pretrained weights for efficiency; train on the new data.
**Car-brand → animals:** *No* — it learned car-specific high-level features, so it won't generalize; *or Yes* if you reuse only the **early layers** (generic low-level features) and retrain the rest.
questions.extend: Reusability decreases with depth: early conv layers (edges) transfer anywhere; the final layers are tied to the source task.

questions.q: You have only 500 labeled medical images. Why fine-tune an ImageNet-pretrained CNN instead of training from scratch, and which layers would you freeze?
questions.answer: 500 images is far too few to train a deep net from scratch (it would overfit). A pretrained backbone already has generic low-level features: **freeze the early conv layers**, **replace the head** for your classes, and **fine-tune the later layers** on your data.
questions.extend: Small data ⇒ reuse generic early features; train only the task-specific head / late layers.

questions.q: For transferring to a 'human present?' road-camera classifier, you have pretrained models on (a) ImageNet, (b) Bird200k, (c) road-scene signs. Which is least suitable, and how would you use the chosen encoder?
questions.answer: **Least suitable: (b) the bird classifier** — a **domain mismatch** between bird images and road images. Using the chosen encoder: **resize inputs** if needed, **add new layers** on top of the embeddings for your output, and **freeze the encoder weights** during training.
questions.extend: Pick a backbone whose pretraining domain is close to the target; freeze it and train only a small new head when data is scarce.

questions.q: Transfer learning with a pretrained ViT (embedding $d=768$) to classify two dog breeds, small dataset, limited GPU. (a) Two techniques to reduce GPU memory. (c) What change to the final layer, and which parameters to train vs freeze and why?
questions.answer: **(a)** Any two: freeze layers, reduce batch size, mixed-precision training, gradient checkpointing. **(c)** Replace the final classification head with a **new layer of output dim 2** (or 1 for binary). **Freeze the ViT backbone** and **train only the new head** — the small dataset would overfit if the whole backbone were tuned, and the pretrained features are already strong (and full fine-tuning is costly).
questions.extend: Small data + big pretrained model ⇒ swap the head, freeze the backbone, train only the head.

questions.q: Using a pretrained ViT's embeddings and a small labeled set, describe a parameter-free way to predict a new test image's label.
questions.answer: Use **k-Nearest-Neighbors** in the embedding space: embed the labeled images and the test image with the frozen ViT, then assign the **majority label of the nearest neighbors** — no parameters are trained.
questions.extend: Frozen-encoder + kNN on embeddings = a strong training-free baseline.
